description: WS1-J labeled fixture corpus for the 0.8.2 intent-artefact classifier. Used to enforce the spec §14.1 cross-domain ship gate: per-domain precision ≥0.80 and recall ≥0.70 across developer / writer / researcher / planner-operator domains. Target ≥20 positive labeled fixtures per domain; negatives establish the false-positive floor.

fixtures.name: dev_deploy_runbook
fixtures.label: intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Production deploy runbook

Follow these steps for every production release:

1. Confirm CI is green on main
2. Tag the release commit with `vX.Y.Z`
3. Push the tag to origin — the release workflow triggers
4. Verify SLSA attestation lands in the release artefacts
5. Promote the new image to production
6. Watch oncall dashboard for 15 minutes
7. Announce in #releases

If step 5 fails, rollback via `kubectl rollout undo` and open an incident.

fixtures.name: dev_env_setup
fixtures.label: intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Development environment setup

1. Clone the repo
2. Install Python 3.12+ and uv
3. Run `uv sync` in the root
4. Copy `.env.example` to `.env` and fill in local secrets
5. Run `make init-db` to seed the local SQLite store
6. Run `pytest tests/ -q` to confirm the suite is green
7. Start the daemon with `vaner daemon run`

fixtures.name: dev_tech_spec
fixtures.label: intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Intent artefacts brief

Goal: let Vaner understand plans, outlines, and task lists as first-class intent signals, not opaque files.

Constraints: local-first, classifier-gated, no wall-clock decay, must generalize beyond software.

Next steps: ship WS1 (ingestion), WS2 (goal integration), WS3 (reconciliation). See release plan for milestones.

fixtures.name: dev_oncall_runbook
fixtures.label: intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Oncall runbook

When the latency alert fires:

1. Check the Grafana dashboard for the affected service
2. Correlate with recent deploys in #deploys
3. If a deploy is the likely cause, initiate rollback
4. If no deploy, check database latency
5. Escalate to DBA oncall if DB is the source
6. Open an incident in Linear
7. Update the public status page if customer-facing

fixtures.name: dev_cicd_plan
fixtures.label: intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# CI pipeline plan

## Stages
1. Lint (ruff, mypy)
2. Unit tests (pytest -q)
3. Integration tests (pytest -m integration)
4. Bench smoke
5. Build wheel
6. SBOM + sigstore sign
7. Publish on tag

## Todo
- [ ] Cache uv downloads between stages
- [ ] Parallelise 2 and 3
- [ ] Add cross-platform matrix

- [ ] How does consensus latency vary under adversarial network conditions?
- [ ] What failure modes do existing algorithms assume?
- [ ] Which metrics best characterise throughput/latency trade-offs?
- [ ] Can the protocol be adapted for geo-distributed clusters?
- [x] Is there a meaningful difference at 10k nodes vs 100?

### Practical deployments (2005-2015)

### Byzantine-tolerant protocols

## Gaps
### Empirical evaluation under partition
### Operational cost at scale

## Our contribution

fixtures.name: researcher_experiment_plan
fixtures.label: intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Experiment plan

## Hypothesis
Consensus latency degrades non-linearly with network jitter.

## Setup
- 5-node cluster, Linux
- inject 10/50/100ms jitter
- 30-minute runs per condition

## Measurements
- [ ] p50, p95, p99 commit latency
- [ ] leader elections per minute
- [ ] throughput in ops/sec

## Analysis
- [ ] fit latency curves
- [ ] regression against jitter

fixtures.name: researcher_methodology
fixtures.label: intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Methodology brief — mixed methods study

Combining quantitative benchmarks and qualitative interviews. Quantitative runs provide the outcome measures; interviews with operators contextualise the failure modes observed. IRB submission drafted; recruitment begins once approval lands.

fixtures.name: researcher_lab_protocol
fixtures.label: intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Lab protocol — cluster experiment

1. Provision 5 identical VMs in same region
2. Install the baseline consensus implementation
3. Configure jitter injection on inter-node links
4. Start the measurement harness
5. Run each jitter level for 30 minutes
6. Archive raw logs to the shared bucket
7. Checksum the archive and update the lab notebook
8. Analyse in the post-run notebook

fixtures.name: planner_incident_runbook
fixtures.label: intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Incident response runbook

When paged:

1. Acknowledge the page within 5 minutes
2. Join the #incident channel
3. Assign an incident commander
4. Begin a running timeline doc
5. Mitigate before investigate
6. Communicate to stakeholders every 30 minutes
7. Declare resolution and schedule postmortem
8. Draft public status update if customer-facing

fixtures.name: planner_support_playbook
fixtures.label: intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Tier-2 support playbook

1. Read the ticket end-to-end before responding
2. Reproduce the issue in the staging environment
3. Check the known-issues doc
4. If novel, escalate to engineering rotation
5. Keep the customer informed every business day
6. On resolution, add the fix pattern to known-issues

## Objective 1 — ship intent artefacts
- KR1.1: release 0.8.2 with WS1-WS3 complete
- KR1.2: cross-domain precision ≥0.80 on the fixture corpus
- KR1.3: adoption uplift ≥+0.3 on the session bench

## Objective 2 — grow usage
- KR2.1: +50 weekly active users
- KR2.2: 3 case studies published

fixtures.name: planner_vendor_migration
fixtures.label: intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Vendor migration runbook — payments

1. Spin up sandbox with the new vendor
2. Run the compatibility test suite
3. Gate the cutover behind a feature flag
4. Enable for 1% of traffic; monitor for 48 hours
5. Ramp to 10%; monitor for 48 hours
6. Ramp to 100%
7. Keep the old vendor as fallback for 14 days
8. Decommission the old integration

# H2 strategy brief

Focus H2 on consolidating 0.8.x into a stable enterprise-ready release. Defer aspirational features to 0.9. Grow revenue through existing pipeline conversion rather than expansion into new segments. Maintain engineering headcount; redirect marketing spend to case studies and community.

fixtures.name: planner_crisis_playbook
fixtures.label: intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Crisis communications playbook

1. CEO and comms lead aligned within 60 minutes of breaking news
2. Draft an initial statement within 2 hours
3. Internal communication goes first — staff learn before public
4. Single spokesperson designated
5. Press outreach coordinated through comms
6. Social response templated before posting
7. Regular updates every 4 hours until resolution
8. Post-crisis retrospective within 2 weeks

fixtures.name: neg_api_docs
fixtures.label: not_intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# `compute()` API reference

The ``compute`` function takes an integer and returns its square. This function is pure and has no side effects. It is thread-safe.

## Parameters

- ``x`` (int): the input value.

## Returns

An integer equal to ``x * x``.

## Exceptions

Raises ``TypeError`` if x is not an int.

fixtures.name: neg_readme
fixtures.label: not_intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Project Vaner

Vaner is a predictive context engine. It runs locally and prepares context packages in the background. The engine uses LLMs and embeddings for retrieval.

## Installation

Install using pip. The default config ships with the package.

## License

Apache 2.0.

fixtures.name: neg_prose_launch
fixtures.label: not_intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Our launch

Today we're excited to announce the launch of our new product. Over the past year, our team has worked tirelessly to bring this vision to reality. We believe it will change how people think about collaboration and knowledge work.

fixtures.name: neg_prose_essay
fixtures.label: not_intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# On patience

Patience is the quiet discipline of staying with a thing. The clockmaker does not hurry the spring. The baker does not rush the rise. In each craft, the work has its own tempo, and the craftsman's role is to keep time with it, not against it.

We live in a culture that mistakes velocity for excellence. A faster release. A shorter loop. A tighter feedback cycle. All useful, none of them the whole story.

fixtures.name: neg_book_review
fixtures.label: not_intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Review — a quiet novel about farms

This is a review, not a plan. The book opens on a muddy field and closes on a frozen one; between them, a year passes in the lives of people whose names we come to know too well. It is a small book that rewards slow reading.

fixtures.name: neg_paper_abstract
fixtures.label: not_intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Abstract

We present empirical measurements of consensus latency in five distributed protocols under controlled jitter conditions. Results show a non-linear degradation above 50ms jitter, with leader elections spiking at 100ms. Implications for practical deployments are discussed.

fixtures.name: neg_paper_intro
fixtures.label: not_intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Introduction

Distributed consensus is a cornerstone of reliable systems. Since Paxos was introduced in 1989, a series of variants have sought to simplify its presentation or strengthen its guarantees. However, empirical measurements of these protocols under adversarial network conditions remain surprisingly sparse.

fixtures.name: neg_syllabus_narrative
fixtures.label: not_intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Course description — distributed systems

This course covers the design and implementation of reliable distributed systems. Topics include time, replication, consensus, consistency models, failure detection, and byzantine faults. Students will complete problem sets and a final project.

fixtures.name: neg_policy_doc
fixtures.label: not_intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Remote work policy

The company supports remote work subject to the following conditions. Employees must be reachable during core hours (10am-3pm in their local time). Physical attendance is required on the first Wednesday of each month. Equipment is provided by the company.

fixtures.name: neg_company_values
fixtures.label: not_intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Our values

## Honesty
We tell the truth, even when it's inconvenient.

## Curiosity
We ask questions before we answer them.

## Care
We consider the people affected by our decisions.

These are not aspirations; they are how we work.

fixtures.name: neg_mission_statement
fixtures.label: not_intent_bearing
fixtures.text:
# Mission

We exist to help people do their best thinking. Our tools exist in service of that goal; when they don't serve it, we change or remove them. We measure ourselves by the quality of the work our users produce, not the time they spend in our products.
